sp.speaker: Philoſtrate.
sp.l: Here mighty Theseus

sp.speaker: Theſeus.
sp.l: Say, what abridgementhaue you for this euening?
What maske, what musicke? how shall we beguile
The lazy tyme,if not with some delight?

sp.speaker: Philoſtrate.
sp.l: There is a briefe, how many sports are rife.
Make choyce, of which your highnesse will see first.

sp.speaker: Theſeus.
sp.l: The battell with the centaures to be sung,
By an Athenian Eunuche, to the harpe?
Weele none of that. That haue I tolde my loue,
In glory of my kinsman Hercules.
The ryot of the tipsie Bachanals,
Tearing the thracian singer, in their rage?
That is an olde deuice: and it was plaid,
When I from Thebes came last a conquerer.
The thrise three Muses, mourning for the death
Of learning,late deceast,in beggery?
That is some Satirekeene and criticall,
Not sorting with a nuptiall ceremony.
A tedious briefe Scene of young Pyramus
And his loue Thisby; very tragicall mirth?
sp.p: Merry, and tragicall? Tedious, and briefe? That is hot Ise, And wōdrous strange snow. How shall we find the cōcord Of this discord?

sp.speaker: Philoſtrate.
sp.l: A Play there is, my Lord, some ten words long;
Which is as briefe, as I haue knowne a play:
But, by ten words, my Lord it is too long:
Which makes it tedious. For in all the Play,
There is not one word apt, one player fitted.
And tragicall, my noble Lord, it is. For Pyramus,
Therein, doth kill himselfe. Which when I saw
Rehearst, I must confesse, made mine eyes water:
But more merry teares the passion of loud laughter
Neuer shed.

sp.speaker: Theſeus.
sp.l: What are they, that doe play it?

sp.speaker: Philoſtrate.
sp.l: Hard handed men, that worke in Athens here,
Which neuerlabour'd in their minds till now:
And now haue toyled their vnbreathed memories,
With this same Play,against your nuptiall.

sp.speaker: Theſeus.
sp.l: And wee will heare it.

sp.speaker: Philoſtrate.
sp.p: No, my noble Lord,it is not for you. I haue heard It ouer, and it is nothing, nothing in the world; Vnlesse you can finde sport in their entents, Extreamely stretcht, and cond with cruell paine, To do you seruice.

sp.speaker: Theſeus.
sp.l: I will heare that play. For neuer any thing
Can be amisse, when simplenesse and duety tender it.
Goe bring them in, and take your places, Ladies.

sp.speaker: Egeus.
sp.l: Here mighty Theseus

sp.speaker: Theſeus.
sp.l: Say, what abridgement haue you for this euening?
What maske, what musicke? how shall we beguile
The lazie tyme, if not with some delight?

sp.speaker: Egeus.
sp.l: There is a breefe how many sports are rife:
Make choise of which your Highnesse will see first.

sp.speaker: Lyſander.
sp.l: The battell with the Centaurs to be sung
By an Athenian Eunuch, to the Harpe.

sp.speaker: Theſeus.
sp.l: Wee'l none of that. That haue I told my Loue
In glory of my kingsman Hercules.

sp.speaker: Lyſander.
sp.l: The riot of the tipsie Bachanals,
Tearing the Thracian singer, in their rage?

sp.speaker: Theſeus.
sp.l: That is an old deuice, and it was plaid
When I from Thebes came last a Conqueror.

sp.speaker: Lyſander.
sp.l: The thrice three Muses, mourning for the death
of learning, late deceast in beggerie.

sp.speaker: Theſeus.
sp.l: That is some Satire keene and criticall,
Not sorting with a nuptiall ceremonie.

sp.speaker: Lyſander.
sp.l: A tedious breefe Scene of young Piramus,
and his loue Thisby; very tragicall mirth.

sp.speaker: Theſeus.
sp.p: Merry and tragicall? Tedious, and briefe? That is, hot ice, and woudrous strange snow. How shall wee finde the concord of this discord?

sp.speaker: Egeus.
sp.l: A play there is, my Lord, some ten words long,
Which is as breefe, as I haue knowne a play;
But by ten words, my Lord, it is too long;
Which makes it tedious: For in all the play,
There is not one word apt, one Player fitted,
And tragicall, my noble Lord, it is; for Piramus
Therein doth kill himselfe. Which when I saw
Rehearst, I must confesse, made mine eyes water:
But more merrie teares,the passion of loud laughter
Neuer shed.

sp.speaker: Theſeus.
sp.l: What are they that do play it?

sp.speaker: Egeus.
sp.l: Hard handed men, that worke in Athens heere,
Which neuer labour'd in their mindes till now;
And now haue toyled their vnbreathed memories
With this same play, against your nuptiall.

sp.speaker: Theſeus.
sp.l: And we will heare it.

sp.speaker: Philoſtrate.
sp.p: No, my noble Lord, it is not for you. I haue heard It ouer, and it is nothing, nothing in the world; Vnlesse you can finde sport their intents, Extreamely strecht, and cond with cruell paine, To doe your seruice.

sp.speaker: Theſeus.
sp.l: I will heare that play. For neuer any thing
Can be amisse, when simplenesse and duty tender it.
Goe bring them in, and take your places, Ladies.

sp.speaker: Egeus.
sp.l: Here mighty Theſeus.

sp.speaker: Theſeus.
sp.l: Say, what Abridgment have you for this Evening?
What Mask? What Muſick? How ſhall we beguile
The lazy time, if not with ſome Delight?

sp.speaker: Egeus.
sp.l: There is a Brief how many Sports are rife:
Make choice of which your Highneſs will ſee firſt.

sp.speaker: Lyſander.
sp.l: The Battel with the Centaur, to be ſung
By an Athenian Eunuch to the Harp.

sp.speaker: Theſeus.
sp.l: We'll none of that. That have I told my Love,
In glory of my Kinſman Hercules.

sp.speaker: Lyſander.
sp.l: The Riot of the tipſie Bachanals,
Tearing the Thracian finger in their rage.

sp.speaker: Theſeus.
sp.l: That is an old device, and it was plaid
When I from Thebes came last a Conqueror.

sp.speaker: Lyſander.
sp.l: The thrice three Muſes mourning for the Death of
Learning, late deceas'd in Beggary.

sp.speaker: Theſeus.
sp.l: That is some Satyr keen and critical,
Not ſorting with a Nuptial Ceremony.

sp.speaker: Lyſander.
sp.l: A tedious brief Scene of young Pyramus,
And his Love Thisby; very tragical Mirth.

sp.speaker: Theſeus.
sp.l: Merry and Tragical? Tedious and Brief? That is,
hot Ice, and wondrous ſtrange Snow. How ſhall we find
the Concord of this Diſcord?

sp.speaker: Egeus.
sp.l: A Play there is, my Lord, ſome ten Words long,
Which is as brief as I have known a Play;
But by ten Words, my lord, it is too long,
Which makes it tedious: For in all the Play
There is not one Word apt, one Player fitted.
And Tragical, my Noble Lord, it is:
For Pyramus therein doth kill himſelf.
Which when I ſaw rehears'd, I must confeſs
Made mine Eyes water; but more merry Tears
The paſſion of loud Laughter never ſhed.

sp.speaker: Theſeus.
sp.l: What are they that do play it?

sp.speaker: Egeus.
sp.l: Hard-handed Men that work in Athens here,
Which never labour'd in their Minds till now;
And now have toil'd their unbreath'd Memories
With this ſame Play, againſt your Nuptials:

sp.speaker: Theſeus.
sp.l: And we will hear it.

sp.speaker: Egeus.
sp.l: No, my Noble lord,
It is not for you. I have heard
It over and it is nothing, nothing in the World,
Unleſs you can find ſport in their Intents,
Extremely ſtretch'd and conn'd with cruel Pain,
To do you Service.

sp.speaker: Theſeus.
sp.l: I will hear that Play: For never any thing
Can be amiſs, when Simpleneſs and Duty tender it.
Go bring them in, and take your Places, Ladies.

sp.speaker: Philoſtrate.
sp.l: Here mighty Theſeus.

sp.speaker: Theſeus.
sp.l: Say, what abridgment have you for this evening?
What mask? what muſick? how ſhall we beguile
The lazy time, if not with ſome delight?

sp.speaker: Philoſtrate.
sp.l: There is a brief how many ſports are riſe:
Make choice of which your Highness will ſee firſt.

sp.speaker: Lyſander.
sp.l: The battel with the Centaurs, to be ſung
By an Athenian eunuch to the harp.

sp.speaker: Theſeus.
sp.l: We'll none of that. That have I told my love,
In glory of my kinſman Hercules.

sp.speaker: Lyſander.
sp.l: The riot of the tipſie Bachanals,
Tearing the Thracian ſinger in their rage.

sp.speaker: Theſeus.
sp.l: That is an old device, and it was plaid
When I from Thebes came laſt a conqueror.

sp.speaker: Lyſander.
sp.l: The thrice three Muſes mourning for the death
Of learning, late deceas'd in beggary.

sp.speaker: Theſeus.
sp.l: That is ſome ſatyr keen and critical,
Not ſorting with a nuptial ceremony.

sp.speaker: Lyſander.
sp.l: A tedious brief ſcene of young Pyramus,
And his love Thisbe; very tragical mirth.

sp.speaker: Theſeus.
sp.l: Merry and tragical? tedious and brief?
How ſhall we find the concord of this diſcord?

sp.speaker: Philoſtrate.
sp.l: A play there is, my lord, ſome ten words long,
Which is as brief as I have known a play;
But by ten words, my lord, it is too long,
Which makes it tedious: for in all the play
There is not one word apt, one player fitted.
And tragical, my noble lord, it is:
For Pyramus therein doth kill himſelf.
Which when I ſaw rehears'd, I muſt confeſs:
Made mine eyes water; but more merry tears
The paſſion of loud laughter never ſhed.

sp.speaker: Theſeus.
sp.l: What are they that do play it?

sp.speaker: Philoſtrate.
sp.l: Hard-handed men that work in Athens here,
Which never labour'd in their minds 'till now;
And now have toil'd their unbreath'd memories
With this ſame play againſt your nuptials:

sp.speaker: Theſeus.
sp.l: And we will hear it.

sp.speaker: Philoſtrate.
sp.l: No, my noble lord,
It is not for you. I have heard it over,
And it is nothing, nothing in the world,
Unleſs you can find ſport in their intents,
Extremely ſtretch'd and conn'd with cruel pain,
To do you ſervice.

sp.speaker: Theſeus.
sp.l: I will hear that play:
For never any thing can be amiſs,
When ſimpleneſs and duty tender it.
Go bring them in, and take your places, ladies.

sp.speaker: Philoſtrate.
sp.l: Here, mighty Theſeus.

sp.speaker: Theſeus.
sp.l: Say, what abridgment have you for this evening?
What maſque? what muſick? how ſhall we beguile
The lazy time, if not with ſome delight?

sp.speaker: Philoſtrate.
sp.l: There is a brief, how many ſports are ripe: 31
Make choice of which your Highneſs will see firſt.

sp.speaker: Theſeus.
sp.l: Theſ. reads.] The battel with the Centaurs,to be ſung 32
By an Athenian eunuch to the harp.
We'll none of that. That I have told my love,
In glory of my kinſman Hercules.
The riot of the tipſie Bacchanals,
Tearing the Thracian ſinger in their rage.
That is an old device; and it was plaid,
When I from Thebes came last a conqueror.
The thrice three Muſes mourning for the death
Of learning, late deceas'd in beggary.
That is ſome ſatyr, keen and critical;
Not ſorting with a nuptial ceremony.
A tedious brief scene of young Pyramus,
And his love Thiſbe; very tragical mirth.
Merry and tragical? tedious and brief?
That is hot Ice, and wondrous ſtrange Snow.
How ſhall we find the concord of this diſcord?

sp.speaker: Philoſtrate.
sp.l: A play there is, my lord, some ten words long;
Which is as brief, as I have known a play;
But by ten words, my lord, it is too long;
Which makes it tedious: for in all the play
There is not one word apt, one player fitted.
And tragical, my noble lord, it is:
For Pyramus therein doth kill himself.
Which, when I ſaw rehears'd, I must confeſs,
Made mine eyes water; but more merry tears
The paſſion of loud laughter never ſhed.

sp.speaker: Theſeus.
sp.l: What are they, that do play it?

sp.speaker: Philoſtrate.
sp.l: Hard-handed men, that work in Athens here,
Which never labour'd in their minds 'till now;
And now have toil'd their unbreath'd memories
With this ſame play againſt your nuptials.

sp.speaker: Theſeus.
sp.l: And we will hear it.

sp.speaker: Philoſtrate.
sp.l: No, my noble lord,
It is not for you. I have heard it over,
And it is nothing, nothing in the world;
Unleſs you can find ſport in their intents,
Extremely ſtretch'd and conn'd with cruel pain,
To do you service.

sp.speaker: Theſeus.
sp.l: I will hear that play:
For never any thing can be amiſs
When simpleneſs and duty tender it.
Go, bring them in, and take your places, ladies.

div.note: ——— how many Sports are rife:] I have chosen to restore from one of the old Quarto's printed in 1600, ripe; as the most proper Word here: ripe, signifying any thing ready for Use; rife, only the great Increase of any thing.

Lys. The battel with the Centaurs—] Here the sixteen Lines, that follow, from the time of the First Folio Edition put out by the Players, have impertinently been divided, by two Verses alternately, betwixt Theseus and Lysander. But what has Lysander to do in the Affair? He is no Courtier of Theseus's, but only an occasional Guest; and just come out of the Woods, so not likely to know what Sports were in Preparation I have taken the old Quarto's for my Guides, in regulating this Passage Theseus asks after Entertainment. Philostrate, who is his Master of the Revels, gives him in a List of what Sports are ready: upon which, Theseus reads the Titles of them out of the List, and then alternately makes his Remarks upon them. And this, I dare say, was the Poet’s own Design and Distribution.

sp.speaker: Philoſtrate.
sp.l: Here, mighty Theſeus.

sp.speaker: Theſeus.
sp.l: Say, what abridgment have you for this evening?
What maſk? what muſick? how ſhall we beguile
The lazy time, if not with ſome delight?

sp.speaker: Philoſtrate.
sp.l: There is a brief how many ſports are ripe:
Make choice of which your Highneſs will fee firſt.

sp.speaker: Theſeus.

l.hi: The battel with the Centaurs, to be ſung

sp.l: By an Athenian eunuch to the harp.
We'll none of that. That have I told my love,
In glory of my kinſman Hercules.
The riot of the tipſie Bacchanals,
Tearing the Thracian ſinger in their rage.
That is an old device, and it; was plaid
When I from Thebes came laſt a conqueror.
The thrice three Muſes mourning for the death.
Of Learning, late deceas'd in beggary.
That is ſome ſatyr keen and critical,
Not ſorting with a nuptial ceremony.
A tedious brief ſcene of young Pyramus,
And his love Thiſbe; very tragical mirth.
Merry and tragical? tedious and brief?
That is hot ice, and wondrous ſcorching ſnow;
How ſhall we find the concord of this diſcord?

sp.speaker: Philoſtrate.
sp.l: A play it is, my lord, ſome ten words long;
Which is as brief as I have known a play;
But by ten words, my lord, it is too long,
Which makes it tedious: for in all the play
There is not one word apt, one player fitted
And tragical, my noble lord, it is:
For Pyramus therein doth kill himſelf.
Which, when I ſaw't rehears'd, I muſt confers
Made mine eyes water; but more merry tears
The paſſion of loud laughter never ſhed

sp.speaker: Theſeus.
sp.l: What are they that do play it?

sp.speaker: Philoſtrate.
sp.l: Hard-handed men that work in Athens here,
Which never labour'd in their minds 'till now;
And now have toil'd their unbreath a memories
With this ſame play againſt your nuptials.

sp.speaker: Theſeus.
sp.l: And we will hear it.

sp.speaker: Philoſtrate.
sp.l: No, my noble lord,
It is not for you. I have heard it over,
And it is nothing, nothing in the world,
Unleſs you can find ſport in their intents,
Extremely ſretch'd and conn'd with cruel pain,
To do you ſervice.

sp.speaker: Theſeus.
sp.l: I will hear that play:
For never any thing can be amiſs,
When ſimpleneſs and duty tender it.
Go, bring them in; and take your places, ladies.
